data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1120 GMT - Bank of America and its customers appear to be navigating greater volatility without any issues for now. CEO Brian Moynihan says business clients have been performing well and consumers have been resilient with continued spending and healthy credit quality. The looming question is whether such conditions can continue amid long-lasting inflation, ramifications of implemented tariffs and weakening confidence in the economy. Among bright spots in Bank of America's 1Q were higher average loans and leases, increased credit/debit card spending and an uptick in consumer checking accounts. "We run our business in a manner intended to withstand volatility for the long-term," says CFO Alastair Borthwick. Shares rise 2% to $37.40 in premarket trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1050 GMT - Gold futures rise close to all-time records on strong safe-haven demand. Futures are up 0.4% at $3,238.40 a troy ounce, close to Friday's record high of $3,263/oz. The precious metal has also gained on reports of the Czech central bank further boosting holdings, alongside the Chinese central bank's higher gold import quotas for commercial banks. With central banks keeping up gold purchases, a continuation of the support seen throughout 2024, the precious metal's next leg higher is being supported by incremental purchases from the investment community, SP Angel analysts say in a note. ETF holdings have climbed again to 89.1 million ounces from 88.7 million ounces on Monday, though this remains well below highs in 2020 and 2022, SP Angel says. As such, ETF demand has room to grow further. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1047 GMT - Indian shares ended higher amid improved sentiment, which found support after the Trump administration announced tariff exemptions for semiconductor equipments, computers and other electronic products over the weekend. The Indian market was closed on Monday for a public holiday. Bank and auto stocks led the gains. IndusInd Bank rose 6.8% and Axis Bank was 4.2% higher. Tata Motors gained 4.5% and Mahindra & Mahindra put up 2.4%. Among the losers, Hindustan Unilever dropped 0.2%. India's Sensex index ended 2.1% higher at 76734.89. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1020 GMT - European natural-gas prices rise in midday trade, with the benchmark Dutch TTF contract up 2.7% at 35.42 euros a megawatt hour. "For now, fundamentals in the European gas market remain supportive," ING analysts say. The bloc ended the 2024-25 heating season with storage levels a little under 34% full, below the five-year average of 45%. "The region will need to see the strongest net injections since 2022. We may need to see more flexibility on these targets, something that member countries are discussing." Prices have been hit by broader risk-off moves in the market last week, and are still around 16% lower on the month. However, analysts say the market is still set to be tight throughout the year and any downward revision to forecast will require a tangible demand hit. ING currently forecasts TTF at 44 euros a megawatt hour. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1019 GMT - TotalEnergies should deliver first-quarter adjusted net income of $4.27 billion, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. Ahead of the French energy major's update, the analysts had expected $4.67 billion. Earnings in its upstream division are still seen at $2.4 billion but its integrated LNG division should report $1.37 billion, down from a prior forecast of $1.53 billion, they write. Refining and chemicals' projected earnings are now $329 million from $423 million, while integrated power is forecast to deliver $488 million from earlier expectations of $637 million, they write. Shares rise 1% to 50.75 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1015 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group's growth profile looks attractive, Deutsche Bank's Benjamin Goy writes in a note. The stock-exchange and financial-information company's revenues are largely recurring, the analyst says. The company has some pricing power, supported by an improving data & analytics product. Additionally, transaction-based revenues benefit from structural growth drivers, amplified by current market volatility, he adds. Therefore, the German bank expects more than 7% revenue growth going forward. DB upgrades its stock recommendation to buy from hold and its target price to 12,600 pence from 11,500 pence. Shares are up 1.6% at 11,255 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1011 GMT - LVMH's results will likely set a negative tone for the 1Q reporting season, Citi analysts Thomas Chauvet and Mahesh Mohankumar write in a note. The performance of the French luxury group, considered a sector bellwether, was overall below the most conservative buy-side expectations, Citi says. With highly elevated economic uncertainty, both in the key U.S. market and globally, it is difficult to build a credible scenario of sequential revenue improvement in the coming two quarters for LVMH and the luxury sector, the analysts say. Consensus expectations for full-year sales and EBIT could be reduced by a low-single-digit and high-single-digit percentage, respectively, they say. Shares trade 7.5% lower at 490.50 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - Publicis delivered a solid first-quarter update that should limit cuts to consensus estimates driven by economic concerns at least for this year, UBS analysts say in a research note. The French advertising group said net revenue grew 4.9% organically in the first quarter, topping expectations of 4.5%. It still expects organic growth of 4% to 5% for the year, with a second-quarter performance within that range. Investors should react positively to the reaffirmation of the 2025 guidance and the new outlook for the second quarter, the analysts say. UBS expects Publicis' second-quarter organic growth to be 4.5%. Shares rise 3.1% to 86.60 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - A U.S. investigation into pharmaceutical imports and potential tariffs in the coming months will likely limit clarity from pharma companies during their 1Q results, Barclays analysts say in a note. The investigation, initiated April 1, aims to assess the national security implications of pharmaceutical imports, including finished generics, nongenerics and active pharmaceutical ingredients, and has to be finalized in around nine months. "With Pharma having been excluded from the initial reciprocal tariffs and it being our house view that some sort of Commerce Department investigation being required for these sectoral tariffs, this isn't too surprising but at least provides some timing framing," they say. Barclays analysts estimate that AstraZeneca, Roche and Grifols appear most insulated, whereas ​GSK and Sandoz look most exposed to a potential tariff risk. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - LVMH's sales miss confirms a tough start to the year, Jefferies analysts write in a note. The luxury conglomerate of French billionaire Bernard Arnault logged sales of 20.31 billion euros for the first quarter, missing Visible Alpha consensus of 21.21 billion euros. Analysts had forecast a challenging start to 2025 due to softening trends in the U.S. after the pickup in the prior quarter. To mitigate the potential hit from U.S. tariffs, LVMH could consider price hikes and an increase in production in its facilities in Texas. Looking ahead, the uncertainty around consumers' willingness to spend at the higher price points in the U.S. and Europe as well as the extent to which Chinese consumers will rebound, will be critical, Jefferies says. Shares fall 7%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - The market is now better prepared for any potential ADR delistings, Julius Baer analysts write in an email. While the market generally assumes that trade tensions between the U.S. and China have peaked, there is still a possibility of Chinese American Depository Receipts being delisted, they add. More Chinese companies have returned to Hong Kong for secondary or dual primary listing compared with that four years ago, they say. Converting ADRs to Hong Kong shares remains a sensible option for investors looking to mitigate the risk of potential delistings, they say. The analysts continue to expect consolidation or correction pressure throughout the second quarter. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0950 GMT - Halfords' business changes are underway, and the company is yielding strong benefits from its strategy, AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould says in a note. The U.K. motoring-and-cycling products provider's integrated service, Fusion, creates cohesion between its retail outlets and its car repair garages. These links are boosting revenue and increasing business resilience given that most people need to keep their cars working, he says. The Fusion segment also provides a solid platform for incoming CEO Henry Birch, who is replacing Graham Stapleton after a seven-year stint, Mould adds. Additionally, the company has found ways to mitigate the impact of higher costs associated with the U.K.'s autumn budget, he says. Shares are up more than 12%. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

data.title: Bond strategists stick to falling yield forecasts despite tariff turmoil
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields will fall, according to bond strategists polled by Reuters who are thinking an economic slowdown in the wake of President Donald Trump's erratic and sweeping tariffs on trading partners will eventually compel the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates.

Their optimism on Treasury market performance comes as inflation expectations surge, creating hesitancy among Fed policymakers on rate cuts, and as nearly half of survey respondents said they were concerned about the market's safe-haven status.

A searing sell-off last week driven by hedge funds unwinding large leveraged bets pushed up the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield by more than 70 basis points to a near two-month high of 4.59%.

While Trump’s surprise 90-day backtrack on reciprocal tariffs, except on China, has since calmed markets investor sentiment has soured considerably. Some have even speculated a large-scale global exodus away from U.S. assets may already be underway.

Nearly half of strategists polled who answered an extra question, 15 of 32, said they were concerned about the safe-haven status of U.S. Treasuries. That compares with slightly more than one-third of FX analysts with similar worries about the dollar in a Reuters poll conducted two weeks ago.

Despite robust demand in a 10-year Treasury auction last week several major U.S. sell-side banks have sounded similar alarms in their recent market commentary.

"The dramatic swings (last) week revealed cracks in the Treasury market that may remain visible for some time," Goldman Sachs strategists wrote in a recent note.

Still, more than 50 bond strategists in an April 10-15 Reuters poll predicted the 10-year yield, currently around 4.38%, would decline to a median 4.21% by the end of June before falling to 4.14% in a year. Forecasts for the 12-month horizon ranged from 3.40% to 5.00%.

Treasury market volatility, measured by the widely regarded MOVE index, shot to an 18-month peak last week and is still more than 50% higher than its long-term average.

"This week, it's my hope, without a lot of confidence, realized volatility comes down a little bit and the intermediate to longer-term themes of disinflation and slowing economic growth in the U.S. start to take hold and pull longer-term yields lower," said Guy LeBas, chief fixed income strategist at Janney Montgomery Scott.

"That said, I have much more confidence in an intermediate-term view which is towards lower yields than I do in a short-term view, which seems to be dominated by overextended positioning," LeBas added.

Fueled by tariffs, consumer inflation expectations have hit their highest in more than 40 years which has effectively tied the Fed’s hands and several officials have advocated for a pause in monetary policy moves until the economic outlook becomes clearer.

Yet interest rate futures are pricing in three Fed rate cuts this year compared with one or two priced at the start of the year.

Bond market strategists appear a little more cautious - 60% of strategists polled, 18 of 30, said risks to their U.S. 10-year yield forecasts were tilted to the upside.

"The tariffs are likely to bring inflation (and) that's making the Fed reticent to cut rates," said Robert Tipp, chief investment strategist at PGIM Fixed Income.

"Slower growth makes a bad fiscal situation worse, plus the federal government is possibly going to change the rules on budgets and allow for current policy to be taken as the baseline. And that could allow for a more lenient budgeting process. All said, this would add a bit of an upward bias to rates."

CEO Brian Moynihan says business clients have been performing well and consumers have been resilient with continued spending and healthy credit quality. The looming question is whether such conditions can continue amid long-lasting inflation, ramifications of implemented tariffs and weakening confidence in the economy. Among bright spots in Bank of America's 1Q were higher average loans and leases, increased credit/debit card spending and an uptick in consumer checking accounts. data.title: Global Carbon Pricing Framework Approved for the Maritime Industry
data.description:
The International Maritime Organization (IMO), the UN agency responsible for regulating international shipping, approved a new global framework for carbon pricing and greenhouse gas fuel standards on April 12, 2025, during its 83rd Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC 83) session. The framework, known as the IMO Net-Zero Framework, marks a pivotal moment for the maritime industry—and one that Climeon views as a significant milestone in the global transition toward climate-aligned shipping.

As the first global emissions pricing mechanism of its kind, this decision sets a precedent for how international regulation can accelerate the shift toward more energy-efficient and lower-emission operations across the global fleet.

IMO Approves First Global Carbon Pricing Mechanism

The newly adopted measures will introduce emissions-based fees for vessels exceeding defined CO₂ thresholds, along with progressive requirements to reduce the greenhouse gas intensity of marine fuels. These initiatives are part of the IMO’s 2023 GHG strategy, which outlines a pathway to achieving net-zero emissions from international shipping by around 2050.

“By putting a price on emissions, this decision reinforces the need for sustainable, energy-efficient operations in the maritime sector,” said Lena Sundquist, CEO. “With emissions tied to operating costs, energy efficiency becomes a strategic priority for shipowners, shipyards, and charterers alike.”

Efficiency Technologies Key to Compliance in Carbon-Priced Shipping

The introduction of global carbon pricing sends a clear economic signal to the maritime sector—reinforcing the importance of onboard energy efficiency and performance as critical levers for compliance and cost control.

In this context, Climeon’s HeatPower technology offers a proven, ready-to-deploy waste heat recovery solution that allows vessels to generate clean electricity from the engines’ cooling water. By reducing fuel consumption and lowering emissions, the technology helps shipowners meet emerging regulatory requirements—while improving overall energy efficiency.

Next Steps: MARPOL Amendment Timeline

The proposed amendments to MARPOL Annex VI, which formalize both the carbon pricing mechanism and the greenhouse gas fuel standard, are scheduled for formal adoption in October 2025, with entry into force expected in 2027, following the IMO’s standard 16-month implementation timeline.

While the overall framework has been agreed, specific pricing levels, emissions thresholds, and implementation details are still under development, and will continue to be refined through ongoing negotiations. As with many international regulatory processes involving a broad base of member states, timelines and details may evolve.

However, the direction is clear—and the momentum behind market-based decarbonization measures continues to grow. Revenue generated through the emissions pricing scheme will fund the newly established IMO Net-Zero Fund, aimed at supporting innovation, infrastructure, and equitable climate action across the maritime sector.

Positioned for a Changing Regulatory Landscape

Climeon sees this development not only as a turning point for sustainable shipping, but also as a key moment for the growing relevance of onboard energy efficiency technologies. With emissions now linked directly to the cost of operations, demand for scalable, fuel-saving solutions is expected to rise.

As the regulatory landscape shifts from ambition to enforcement, Climeon is well-positioned to support the maritime industry in achieving both compliance and long-term decarbonization goals.

data.title: How Trump's tariffs could leave the Fed at risk of triggering stagflation
data.description:
By Peter Morici

Appeasing Trump would require the Fed to abandon its 2% inflation target. Pressure is mounting for that.

During the 2024 presidential campaign, then-candidate Donald Trump promised to cut taxes and regulations, and to impose tariffs. Wall Street and business leaders - wrongly, as it turned out - assumed that tax- and regulatory relief would arrive first, and tariffs would be imposed only after U.S. efforts to negotiate better terms of trade with Europe, China and others.

No one seriously considered that President Trump would mount a wholesale assault on the global supply chain, with big tariffs on key suppliers from Canada to Vietnam.

Those tariffs have come quickly, while deregulation is a slow process. Tax cuts depend on Congress and likely won't become law much before 2026. The U.S. Federal Reserve, meanwhile, is being challenged to pull down core inflation. The consumer price index less food and energy rose 2.8% in March, largely due to persistent price and cost pressures in the services sector.

Even with Trump's 90-day suspension of many of the largest reciprocal tariffs, the average duty on imports to the U.S. is up about 20 percentage points since Inauguration Day. This will reverse recent progress on goods inflation. The impact won't be a one-off, either - despite what U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent contends. Rather, it will be inflationary - embedded in workers' wage demands.

At the same time, Trump's tax cuts won't really increase U.S. corporate investment or economic productivity. The 2017 Jobs Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, which set the corporate tax rate at 21%, made U.S. business taxes more than competitive with other major world economies. Slashing the tax rate further, to 15%, won't do much more good. Exemptions for worker tips and Social Security benefits will distort labor markets.

The Fed will continue to be challenged in that environment. Getting inflation below 3% again will likely require the Fed to raise interest rates to slow the economy - but at a time when the economy is already under pressure from tariffs, raising the odds of a recession.

Abandoning the 2% inflation target

Appeasing Trump would require the Fed to implicitly abandon its 2% target - and pressure is mounting for that.

All along, Powell has pursued an economic "soft landing" - inflation near-target but without a recession. But at a congressional hearing in February, Senator John Kennedy (R-LA) said Powell should take credit for accomplishing a soft landing - implying that the new U.S. inflation target is just below 3%.

Before COVID, U.S. inflation averaged 1.5% for more than a decade. The Fed faced pressure from progressive economists to adopt a higher target. Lately more moderate commentators have joined that chorus.

Bloomberg columnist John Authers has suggested the current pace of inflation is " now within the 3% upper range of the Federal Reserve's target." That's curious, because the Fed hasn't expressed its target in terms of a range.

Economist Mohamed A. El-Erian has suggested that structural conditions in the U.S. economy have sufficiently changed since the pre-COVID period to justify a higher target. He notes that 2% was a somewhat arbitrary choice without sufficient analytical underpinnings.

Read: Fed's Waller says interest rates may need to be cut soon if largest Trump tariffs stay in place

Sticking to the target

The Fed is charged with accomplishing both price stability and maximum employment, and we should look to former Fed Chair Alan Greenspan for guidance: "Price stability," he said, "is that state in which expected changes in the general price level do not effectively alter business or household decisions."

Otherwise, consumers would constantly try to accelerate purchases of big items like appliances and cars, and inflation would rise ever higher. Now, with U.S. inflation running at 2.8% annually and Trump implementing much higher tariffs, American consumers are expecting much higher inflation. The most recent University of Michigan survey recorded a one-year expectation of 6.7%.

The Federal Reserve will have to choose. The U.S. economy was already bound to slow even before Trump's tariffs. If the president continues with the tariffs he's implemented, the U.S. will likely see economic growth decline to about 1% or less while inflation accelerates.

At 1% growth, unemployment would rise and the white-collar worker problem - middle managers and administrators facing shrinking opportunities due to the increased use of artificial intelligence - will worsen.

Yet at that point, if the Fed acts to reflate the economy, inflation could easily rise above 4%. But if the Fed enables Trump's reckless macroeconomic and tariff policies and fails to put the brakes on inflation, the U.S. could lose grasp of price stability for a decade or more. Stagflation becomes a significant risk.

Peter Morici is an economist and emeritus business professor at the University of Maryland, and a national columnist.

More: Will reduced AI regulation spur innovation or increase risk?

Also read: It's rare for stocks and the dollar to fall together. History says tread carefully.

-Peter Morici

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Fed's Waller Forecasts Sustained Tariffs Could Send Inflation Near 5%
data.description:
Fed's Waller Forecasts Sustained Tariffs Could Send Inflation Near 5% By Vicky Ge Huang

Fed governor Christopher Waller called new tariffs "one of the biggest shocks to affect the U.S. economy in many decades."

Waller forecasts that if they stay high they could temporarily lift inflation to near 5%, slow economic growth and prompt more rate cuts.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he and President Trump are considering who would lead the Federal Reserve next.

And Bridgewater Associates founder Ray Dalio said the U.S. economy could easily be pushed into a recession because of Trump's trade war.

Top News Fed's Waller: Sustained Tariffs Could Send Inflation Near 5%

In a speech in St. Louis, Fed governor Christopher Waller acknowledged high uncertainty about the future path of trade policy and the economic consequences.

"I have struggled, like many others I have talked with, to fit these varying possibilities into a single coherent view of the outlook," Waller said.

But he offered some of the most specific projections to date from a Fed official about how the new tariffs could harm the economy in the short term if they stay high. In addition to boosting inflation, high tariffs could raise unemployment to near 5% and cool the economy, Waller said.

If resulting inflation proves temporary, the risk of a recession could prove to be the Fed's bigger priority, Waller said. In that scenario, "I would expect to favor cutting the FOMC's policy rate sooner, and to a greater extent than I had previously thought," Waller said, referring to the Federal Open Market Committee that sets monetary policy.

Bessent Thinks About Next Federal Reserve Chair 'All The Time'

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he and President Trump are considering who would lead the Federal Reserve next. "We think about it all the time," Bessent said in a Bloomberg TV interview Monday afternoon. He said interviews with potential candidates will start in the fall. The term of current Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell ends in May 2026.

Hedge-Fund Founder Ray Dalio: 'Very Close to a Recession'

Ray Dalio, the founder of hedge-fund firm Bridgewater Associates, said Sunday that the U.S. economy could easily be pushed into a recession because of President Trump's trade war.

U.S. Economy Apartment Developers Who Overbuilt Luck Out With Tariffs

Multifamily owners are slogging through a historic glut of new supply . The biggest wave of apartment construction in 40 years has pressured rent prices, forcing landlords to offer months of free rent and other incentives to fill their units.

Trump Gave Automakers a Tariff Break. It's Causing More Confusion.

The White House sought to give car companies a break on a hefty auto tariff enacted this month by offering a deduction for American-made parts.

Trump Govt Retreats From White-Collar Criminal Enforcement

Gordon Coburn and Steven Schwartz were on the verge of going to trial on charges of scheming to pay bribes in India when President Trump issued an executive order that put enforcement of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act on hold..

Forward Guidance Tuesday (all times ET)

8:30 a.m.: Import & Export Price Indexes

8:30 a.m.: Empire State Manufacturing Survey

9 a.m.: Johnson Redbook Retail Sales Index

Wednesday

9:45 a.m.: Bank of Canada interest rate announcement

10 a.m.: Manufacturing & Trade: Inventories & Sales

10 a.m.: NAHB Housing Market Index

12 p.m.: Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland President Beth Hammack speaks at Columbus Metropolitan Club Weekly Forum event

1:30 p.m.: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell speaks at the Economic Club of Chicago

7 p.m.: FRB Dallas President Lorie Logan and FRB Kansas City President Jeffrey Schmid speak at Global Perspectives event

Research Deutsche Bank Strategists Expect Fed to Step In as Tariff Shocks Weigh on Economy

Deutsche Bank's U.S. economists had been holdouts on Wall Street in recent months-one of the few investment-bank research teams that forecast the Fed would hold rates steady through all of 2025. But tariffs that look more severe than initially expected now mean the Fed is more likely to act to cushion the economy, Deutsche economist Matthew Luzzetti and his colleagues write. "The trade policy announcements represent a significant adverse supply shock to the economy," they write. Deutsche Bank now expects the Fed to respond with a quarter-point rate cut in December, followed by two more cuts in January and March of next year. - Matt Grossman

ECB Could Cut Interest Rates Three More Times in 2025

The European Central Bank's deposit rate--currently at 2.50%--is likely to fall to 1.75% by the end of the year, implying three interest-rate cuts, says private bank Merck Finck's Robert Greil in a note. Merck Finck's expectation is in line with money market pricing. "In our view, the eurozone faces less of a threat from the inflation side than from the growth side," the chief strategist says. If China now barely exports any goods to the U.S. in light of exorbitant tariffs, Europe may be "flooded" with more--and cheaper--goods from China, he says. "The associated price effect could cause inflation to approach the ECB's medium-term target of 2% even faster than expected." - Emese Bartha

Rising Risks Spur Downgrades to China Growth Projections

Several investment banks have cut growth projections for China, seeing bigger-than-expected economic risks from U.S. tariffs. UBS on Tuesday lowered expectations for China's economic growth to 3.4% this year from 4% previously, a far cry from Beijing's annual target of around 5%. Economists at the Swiss bank anticipate that China's exports to the U.S. will plunge by two-thirds in the coming quarters, with overall exports sliding 10% in dollar terms this year. In 2026, China's economic growth could slow to just 3%, they said in a report. Goldman Sachs and Citigroup also cut growth views on China in recent days, citing similar concerns about tariffs announced by the Trump administration. President Trump's unpredictable policy announcements have clouded outlook for the world's second-largest economy, with Beijing also hitting back with its own tariffs on the U.S. - Dow Jones Newswires

Basis Points Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is prioritizing the U.K., Australia, South Korea, India and Japan as among his top targets for new trade deals after President Trump announced a wave of tariffs including on those countries, according to people familiar with the matter. The nascent recovery in Canada's housing market has become a casualty of the trade dispute with the U.S. Wage growth in the U.K. picked up in the first few months of the new year , suggesting that continued high inflation will remain a concern for the Bank of England, despite the damping effect on growth likely to come from U.S. trade tariffs. The outlook for the German economy cratered this month after U.S. President Trump's tariff blitz, according to a monthly survey of financial analysts. President Trump's escalating tariff spat with China threatens to push Chinese consumers even further into the arms of domestic brands . Chinese port activity slumped in early April , with data indicating that exports to the U.S. have been hammered by President Trump's tariffs. At the world's biggest wholesale market in this eastern Chinese exporting hub, American clients have disappeared . Japan is ready to help the U.S. achieve its economic goals , but in a way that's mutually beneficial, Japan's economy minister said ahead of trade talks with the Trump administration. South Korea's government is planning to increase support for chip makers to around $23 billion, proposing a bigger-than-expected supplementary budget that will also dial up financial aid for the broader economy and wildfire disaster relief. Oil demand growth is forecast to be weaker than previously projected as escalating global trade tensions sparked by President Trump's tariffs weigh on the global economy, the International Energy Agency said. The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries cut its forecast for oil demand growth just days after a surprise decision to boost output, citing the impact of U.S. tariffs on the global economy and crude consumption. About Us

WSJ Pro Central Banking brings you central banking news, analysis and insights from WSJ's global team of reporters and editors. This newsletter was compiled by markets reporter Vicky Ge Huang in New York. Send your tips, suggestions and feedback to [vicky.huang@wsj.com].

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Bond market is the Achilles' heel of Trump's 'big, beautiful' budget bill, say investors
data.description:
By Joy Wiltermuth

The U.S. bond market sent an ominous message to the White House in recent days, but it wasn't only about President Donald Trump's tariffs.

The "freak selloff" in government bonds followed Trump's April 2 widespread tariffs against major U.S. trading partners - and some of the biggest buyers of American debt.

Yet the bond market already was set to be the elephant in the room as Trump's "big beautiful" budget bill heads for a potential showdown in Congress, including over plans for a multitrillion-dollar tax-cut package.

"The tone seems very dependent on any given headline or hour," said Mike Cudzil, a portfolio manager at Pimco, one of the world's largest asset managers, about the U.S. bonds on Monday. "The mood is extremely fluid."

Stocks closed higher for a second straight session Monday and bond yields eased back from last week's extreme selloff, helped by Trump's promise to give a reprieve to some tech products from tariffs on China.

Tumult in bonds prompted Boston Federal Reserve President Susan Collins on Friday to say the U.S. central bank was "absolutely" ready to step in and intervene, if needed, to calm financial markets.

For now, markets feel less on edge, Cudzil said, even though he wasn't ruling out future sharp yield moves, given the on-off tariff backdrop and looming budget bill that could keep the U.S. budget deficit at pretty high levels for years to come.

"It seems the biggest bully in the room is the bond market," Cudzil said.

Bonds and politics

Investors warned that recent violent moves in the $29 trillion Treasury market gave the Trump administration a taste of what other governments have already faced.

U.K. Prime Minister's Liz Truss's government in 2022 sparked a mini budget crisis that forced a pivot, and eventually left her out of a job. James Carville, a Democratic political adviser, years ago said that if reincarnated, he would want to come back as the bond market, because it can intimidate anyone.

MarketWatch reported in November that bond buyers already were bracing for a potential battle over plans and policies that risked adding to the U.S. debt load, or harming America's ability to borrow at favorable rates on the open market.

Trump's tariffs have been stoking fear of a pullback - or outright selling - of U.S. debt from international buyers. But so far in 2025, they remain "firm buyers" of long-dated Treasury bonds, or maturities of at least 10 years, Geoff Yu, EMEA macro strategist at BNY, said in a Monday client note. BNY oversees more than $53 trillion in assets, giving it a view into global investors' fund flows.

"It's nice to see things chilling a little bit," John Luke Tyner, head of fixed income at Aptus Capital Advisors, told MarketWatch on Monday, saying his hope was for more market calm, and time for the administration to encourage a return of more manufacturing to the U.S.

Trump's 'Achilles' heel'

Tyner also said the calmer vibes may not last long, given the estimated $7.5 trillion of U.S. bonds coming due over the next few years, as well as potentially a couple of trillion of dollars more of new debt stemming from the budget bill.

While Congress has begun a two-week recess, Trump's budget bill could face obstacles upon the return of lawmakers to Washington in the final week of April. There's a goal of producing a budget deal for Trump to sign by Memorial Day.

Matthew Eagan, a portfolio manager at Loomis, Sayles & Co., said the bond market could end up being Trump's "Achilles' heel" as those negotiations get under way, given his tariff fight and uncertainty around how much additional U.S. debt it could require. "These auctions are going to keep coming every month," Eagan said of the ongoing sales of Treasurys.

The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y eased back to 4.361% on Monday, while the 30-year yield BX:TMUBMUSD30Y fell 7.8 basis points to 4.796% after logging its biggest weekly gain since 1987.

If U.S. bonds weren't closely watched at the White House before, Trump let everyone know they were now on his radar.

"The bond market is very tricky," Trump said on April 9, as he paused sweeping tariffs announced only a week before. "I was watching it," he said, of U.S. bonds following a smooth 10-year auction. "The bond market right now is beautiful," he added.

The U.S. Treasury market matters for its "haven" status and because it has been stitched into the fabric of global trade and finance. Closer to home, it also helps set what America's middle-class ends up paying for mortgages, car loans and much more, making it politically important.

Longer Treasury rates initially fell in early April after Trump surprised the world with higher-than-expected levels of "reciprocal" tariffs. But that was mainly because they raised alarms that his trade fight could tip the U.S. economy into a recession.

Then, roughly a week ago, bond yields quickly made a U-turn higher, catching traders off guard and exposing cracks in the market's functions. Those concerns haven't gone away, but Trump's pivot to a more flexible stance on tariffs has given investors a reprieve from the recent chaos.

Read: Fed's Waller says interest rates may need to be cut soon if largest Trump tariffs stay in place

"The risk is that one of these smaller, one-off liquidity events triggers a bigger move that's not contained in 24 hours - and it might break something," said Bill Campbell, global bond portfolio manager at DoubleLine.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA ended Monday 10% off its record close in early December, while the S&P 500 index SPX was 12% off its previous peak and the Nasdaq Composite Index COMP was 16.6% lower, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

-Joy Wiltermuth

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Capitalreorganisation and transfer of shares
data.description:
a.i.s. AG

by Dr. Johan Bendien

Business news for the stock market

a.i.s. AG: Capitalreorganisation and transfer of shares

Berlin (pta000/15.04.2025/13:15 UTC+2)

Berlin: a.i.s. AG has been successful in restructuring the company in recent months. It is intended to expand the business segment by changing the shareholder structure, which will enable the company to reposition itself in terms of sales. Additional capital measures are planned that will promote the development and corporate capital of a.i.s. AG. This development runs parallel to our previous plans and announcements.

Over the past 11 months, we have been able to motivate many shareholders to transfer 73.5% of their shares to the rightful owner. These shareholders will be able to participate in the company's development over the next few years and benefit from its growth.

On the other hand, 2.9 million shares have still not been transferred to the owner. We have reported extensively on this in various press releases. The company will therefore consider these shares to be unlawfully held by the shareholders after 30 April 2025 and will derecognise them from its share capital. At the same time, the shareholders of these shares will also lose their legal rights as shareholders with their entire shareholdings from 1 May 2025. In general, these shareholders will then lose their voting rights and participation in shareholders' meetings. A last opportunity is hereby granted to avoid the complete loss of the shares by transferring the 73.5% of their holdings to the owner shareholder's securities account by 16 May 2025, 16:00.

We continue to reserve the right to take legal action against the credit institutions involved, as they have not supported the necessary measures in a targeted manner. The process will be presented on our company website www.https://ais-ag.eu/index.php?page=98 and adjusted in terms of time.

15.04.2025

(end)

Emitter:a.i.s. AGFriedrichstraße 17110117 BerlinGermany
Contact Person:Dr. Johan Bendien
Phone:+49 30 30366 0502
E-Mail:h.bendien@ais-ag.eu
Website:www.ais-ag.eu
ISIN(s):DE0006492903 (Share)
Stock Exchange(s):Regulated Market in Frankfurt; Free Market in Berlin, Dusseldorf, Stuttgart

data.title: Fitch upgrades Pakistan's credit rating to 'B-' on improving deficits and reforms
data.description:
Global ratings agency Fitch on Tuesday upgraded Pakistan's foreign currency credit rating to 'B-' from 'CCC+', citing increased confidence in the country's progress on narrowing its budget deficits.

The upgrade also reflects confidence that the country would implement structural reforms, supporting its International Monetary Fund (IMF) programme performance and funding availability, Fitch said.

The agency said though ongoing global trade tensions could create external pressures on Pakistan, its low dependence on exports and market financing should mitigate risks.

Pakistan's economy had been teetering on the brink of default ever since inflation rose to a record high in May 2023 and reserves started shrinking, but has seen some respite thanks in part to a $7 billion bailout programme from the IMF.

In March, the IMF reached a new deal with Pakistan which could unlock $1.3 billion in cash.

data.title: Palm oil falls on estimates of improved output, weak rivals
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures fell for a second consecutive session on Tuesday, weighed down by expectations for improved production, while weaker Dalian vegetable oils and Chicago soyoil added pressure.

The benchmark palm oil contract for June delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange lost 63 ringgit, or 1.51%, to 4,107 ringgit ($931.29) a metric ton at closing, its lowest closing since Sept. 30.

"Moving forward, production is expected to pick up both locally and in Indonesia, thus the selling pressure that follows," a Kuala Lumpur-based trader said, adding that export data was supportive of the price.

Exports of Malaysian palm oil products for April 1-15 estimated to rise by 17% from a month ago, said cargo surveyor Intertek Testing Services, while independent inspection company AmSpec Agri Malaysia estimated exports grew by 13.6%.

Dalian's most-active soyoil contract (DBYcv1) fell 0.23%, while its palm oil contract was down 0.39%.

Soyoil prices on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) (BOc2) slipped 0.26%.

Palm oil tracks the price movements of rival edible oils as it competes for a share of the global vegetable oils market.

Palm oil may retest support at 4,072 ringgit per metric ton, a break below could trigger a drop to 4,026 ringgit, Reuters technical analyst Wang Tao said.

($1 = 4.4100 ringgit)

data.title: Kremlin, asked about reported request to base Russian aircraft in Indonesia, declines comment
data.description:
The Kremlin, asked on Tuesday about a media report that Russia has asked Indonesia for permission to base its aircraft on its territory, declined to comment, saying there was a lot of "fake news" around.

Defence publication Janes reported that Jakarta had received an official request from Moscow, seeking permission for Russian Aerospace Forces (VKS) aircraft to be based at a facility in Indonesia's easternmost province of Papua.

In a call with reporters, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said of the report: "There are a lot of different pieces of fake news around, publications in the media, including those that relate to sensitive areas. But in this case we are not commenting on such publications."

Australia's Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said his government had asked Indonesia about the report, as the issue dominated Australia's election campaign on Tuesday.

Australian Defence Minister Richard Marles has spoken to his Indonesian counterpart who said there would be no Russian air force planes based in Indonesia, according to a report by the Australian Broadcasting Corporation.

Russia has friendly relations with Indonesia, with which it conducted joint naval exercises last year. Russian Security Council Secretary Sergei Shoigu visited Indonesia to discuss security and defence issues in February.

Russian Deputy Prime Minister Denis Manturov met Indonesia’s President Prabowo Subianto at the presidential palace in Jakarta on Tuesday, according to a local television report.

data.title: EU Trade Chief Discussed Global Oversupply of Metals With U.S. Officials
data.description:
By Kim Mackrael

The European Union's trade chief discussed concerns about a global oversupply of steel and aluminum products during a meeting with U.S. trade officials in Washington on Monday.

Maros Sefcovic, the EU trade commissioner, met with U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to kick off negotiations. The EU hopes the talks can result in a resolution to U.S. tariffs.

The officials also discussed the bloc's suggestion that both sides eliminate all industrial-goods tariffs for each others' imports, as well as the resilience of semiconductor and pharmaceutical supply chains, an EU spokesman said in a statement.

The EU said significant joint efforts would be needed to reach a deal during the U.S.'s 90-day pause on some tariffs. The bloc continues to prepare retaliatory measures in case negotiations fail.

"The EU is doing its part. Now, it is necessary for the U.S. to define its position," the statement said.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Spruce Biosciences Announces New Corporate Strategy And Acquisition Of Tralesinidase Alfa
data.description:
Spruce Biosciences Inc :

  • SPRUCE BIOSCIENCES ANNOUNCES NEW CORPORATE STRATEGY AND ACQUISITION OF TRALESINIDASE ALFA FOR THE TREATMENT OF SANFILIPPO SYNDROME TYPE B (MPS IIIB)

  • SPRUCE BIOSCIENCES INC - BLA SUBMISSION TO FDA FOR TA-ERT ANTICIPATED IN 1H 2026

  • SPRUCE BIOSCIENCES INC: EXPECTS ITS CASH RUNWAY TO FUND ITS CURRENT OPERATING PLAN THROUGH END OF 2025
